notes: Round 2 research covering late February through March 4, 2026. Focused on new moratoriums, bans, lawsuits, protests, legislative actions, and zoning restrictions discovered via web search. Cross-referenced against existing fights.json (345 entries) and new-fights-march2026.json to avoid duplicates.

new_entries.summary: Urbana City Council voted 6-1 to approve a 12-month moratorium on data centers over 10,000 sq ft, halting a proposed 460,000 sq ft Thor Equities facility off Routes 55 and 68. Over 40 citizens spoke at the meeting. The site consists of three parcels purchased for $5 million. Concerns center on impacts to Cedar Bog, water, electricity, noise, and property values. The only dissenting vote came from the mayor's wife.

Royalton voters approved a five-year moratorium on AI and crypto data centers via show-of-hands vote at Town Meeting Day. About 154 of the town's registered voters participated. No data centers have been proposed for the town; the ban is preemptive. The Planning Commission will write the policy into the town plan for Selectboard approval. State Senator Becca White spoke at the meeting about her statewide moratorium bill (S. 205).

Elmwood Township Board unanimously approved a 9-month moratorium on data centers (reduced from originally proposed 12 months). No known data center plans exist for the county; the moratorium is precautionary. The board tabled the measure at its January 21 meeting before passing the amended version in February.

Ann Arbor Township Board of Trustees passed a 12-month moratorium on data centers. No developer has approached township officials about a data center; the moratorium is a proactive measure reflecting concerns about water, energy demand, and environmental impacts seen in neighboring communities.

Anchorage Assembly held public hearing on Ordinance AO 2026-27 to amend Title 21 of the Municipal Code, defining data centers as a distinct land use and restricting them to conditional approval in industrial, port, airport, and public lands zones. Requires landscaping buffers, setbacks, enclosed power equipment, and detailed impact reviews. Current definitions date to the 1990s. Next public hearing scheduled March 24.

Mooringsport Mayor Tyler Gordon, Michael Craft, and Mary Blakemore filed a petition for judicial review in Caddo District Court challenging the Shreveport City Council's approval of a special use permit for a TXLAAR LLC data center. The lawsuit alleges the city violated transparency laws by using NDAs, and that the facility would use up to 7.5 million gallons of water/day and discharge 2.5 million gallons of contaminated wastewater daily. The Sierra Club Delta Chapter supports the lawsuit and is working to join as co-plaintiffs. The facility would include seven 400,000 sq ft buildings.

Rural communities in North Caddo Parish organized a boycott of the official data center community meeting in Blanchard on March 3. Residents near Caddo Lake oppose a proposed data center campus on State Line Road and Blanchard Latex Road near Mooringsport. State Rep. Danny McCormick has demanded transparency around NDAs signed by local officials. The Village of Mooringsport unanimously approved a resolution on Feb. 12 calling for an environmental study. Caddo Commission declined to vote on a one-year water moratorium proposed by Commissioner Chris Kracman.

After Independence City Council voted 5-2 on March 2 to approve $150.6 billion in Chapter 100 bonds and 90% tax abatements for Nebius's hyperscale AI data center in the Little Blue Valley, residents immediately launched a referendum petition. The group filed intent with over 100 signatures at the city clerk's office and needs 150 verified signatures to proceed. 49 of 67 speakers at the meeting opposed the project. 'Stop the AI Data Center in Independence' Facebook group has nearly 2,000 members. The group hopes to get a question on the August 2026 ballot.
opposition_groups: Stop the AI Data Center in Independence

new_entries.summary: A conditional use hearing for a 120 MW data center as part of a $3 billion 'Armory Innovation District' in Midtown St. Louis is scheduled for March 19 before the Zoning Section. Developer David Lambiaso proposes converting the adjacent Macy's/Famous Barr warehouse into a data center while the historic Armory becomes office space. Residents pushed back at a Planning Commission meeting, saying no to data centers within the city. City simultaneously developing new zoning rules to restrict where data centers can be built. Breathe Better STL is among opposition groups.

PA House Energy Committee advanced two data center bills on party-line 14-12 votes. HB 2150 (Rep. Mullins, Lackawanna County) requires annual water/energy consumption reports to the state DEP. HB 2151 (Rep. Donahue, Lackawanna County) directs DCED to develop a model zoning ordinance for municipalities to regulate data centers. All Democrats voted in support; all Republicans opposed. Part of broader PA resistance movement with 50+ proposed data centers facing grassroots opposition statewide.

Bipartisan House Bill 706 (Rep. Rader, D-Lakewood and Rep. Thomas, R-Jefferson) would extend AEP Ohio's data center tariff model statewide. Requires data centers to sign minimum 12-year contracts with utilities before infrastructure construction begins, bans utilities from passing data center infrastructure costs to other customers, and mandates financial assurances and exit/unused-capacity charges. AEP's existing tariff requires commitment to cover 85% of projected energy use even if less is consumed.

WV House of Delegates passed HB 4983 (78-16) establishing rules for the Department of Commerce to certify microgrids and high-impact data centers. The House rejected amendments requiring water usage transparency and public nuisance protections. Environmental groups including WV Rivers Coalition and League of Women Voters of WV urged lawmakers to add water-use protections. The bill now moves to the Senate. Follows 2025's HB 2014 which created the certified microgrid program.
opposition_groups: League of Women Voters of WV

new_entries.summary: Bipartisan group of Michigan lawmakers introduced House Bills 5594-5596 to place a one-year statewide moratorium on data center construction, prohibiting new data centers from receiving state or local permits until April 1, 2027. Led by Rep. Jennifer Wortz (R). At least 27 Michigan communities now have active local moratoria. However, the legislation is considered unlikely to pass under House Speaker Matt Hall and Governor Whitmer, who have opposed previous efforts.

Food & Water Watch released 'The Urgent Case Against Data Centers,' a comprehensive first-of-its-kind report making the case for a nationwide moratorium on new AI-driven data center construction. Released the same day President Trump hosted a White House signing ceremony with seven Big Tech companies pledging to reduce AI energy impacts. Report highlights water/power consumption, fossil fuel demand, air/water pollution risks, loss of farmland, and noise pollution.

Delta County Planning Commission considered a resolution to suspend data center and cryptocurrency mining projects for 12 months while a zoning ordinance is developed. Commission Chair Amy Berglund said no known data center plans exist for the county but cited concerns over utility demand and water usage. Final decision expected in April 2026.

updates.summary: Lordstown rescinded its November 2025 data center ban and replaced it with a 180-day moratorium after legal advice that Ohio courts favor moratoriums over outright bans. Bristolville LLC is fighting the village in Ohio Supreme Court, claiming their data center application was filed Oct. 20 before the ban. Council approved $400 for a consultant (Brian Frantz, Richfield Village planning director) to present on data center impacts at a future meeting.

Commission voted unanimously on March 3 to extend the data center moratorium to June 5, 2026 (originally set to expire March 6). Commissioners also unanimously voted to hold text amendments and return them to the planning commission for revision. The amendments would define 'data center' in the zoning code and set regulations. Commissioner Tiffany Taylor has supported an outright ban on data centers.

Birmingham City Council unanimously approved a 180-day moratorium on March 3 on applications for new or expanding hyperscale data centers (20+ MW). Two existing projects grandfathered: Nebius and DC Block. The pause gives the city time to review zoning, assess infrastructure demands, and study best practices. Planning commission workshop tentatively March 11, public hearing March 18, potential new regulations vote by April 28.

Ypsilanti City Council approved both a 60-day emergency moratorium and a 365-day resolution on data center zoning permits. The emergency ordinance takes immediate effect. This is a separate action from the earlier Ypsilanti Township petition effort.

Fulton County Commissioners voted 2-1 to approve a one-year moratorium on data center construction, effective immediately. Follows months of debate and strong opposition from residents near Akron concerned about water and electricity impacts. A committee will be formed to review data center impacts; the Area Plan Commission is expected to appoint members at its March 23 meeting.

TigerDC formally withdrew Project Spero, a planned $3 billion AI data center at Tyger River Industrial Park along Highway 290. The withdrawal came after Spartanburg County Council unanimously voted down two Fee-In-Lieu-Of-Tax (FILOT) agreements in a special meeting on Feb. 27. The project would have employed 50 people and paid an estimated $249 million in taxes over 30 years. Widespread resident opposition and Council refusal to approve tax incentives sealed the project's fate.

Recall effort against Mayor Ted Neitzke failed, falling several hundred signatures short of the 25% threshold by the Feb. 16 deadline. However, Great Lakes Neighbors United's separate lawsuit challenging $458M in TIF financing (TID) continues, with a motion hearing scheduled for March 13. The group also submitted a referendum petition requiring voter approval for future TIDs over $10M. Business associations counter-sued over the referendum ordinance. The $15B data center campus (Vantage Data Centers/OpenAI/Oracle/Stargate) construction is underway.

Independence City Council voted 5-2 on March 2 to approve $150.6 billion in Chapter 100 bonds and 90% tax abatements ($6.6B total) for Nebius's 2+ million sq ft hyperscale AI data center in the Little Blue Valley at Little Blue Pkwy/MO-78. Mayor Rory Rowland and Councilmember Brice Stewart voted against. Residents immediately launched a referendum petition to force a public vote, aiming for the August 2026 ballot.

Linn County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved one of the nation's strictest local data center zoning ordinances on Feb. 18. Requirements include comprehensive water study, formal water-use agreement before construction, 1,000-foot residential setbacks, noise and light pollution limits, road damage compensation, community betterment fund contributions, and a new data-center-specific zoning district. County planning director says no Iowa ordinance asks for more requirements.

Charles County Planning Commission voted on March 2 to recommend denial of Zoning Text Amendment #25-187, which would have established zoning regulations for data centers in the county. The denial recommendation will go to the County Commissioners for final decision.

Mason City Council reviewed a referendum petition with 700+ signatures seeking to repeal Ordinance 266 (M-3 Technology Innovation zoning district) passed Feb. 2. Residents say the ordinance is 'dangerously inadequate' and doesn't sufficiently address lithium battery usage and noise concerns. The council is waiting on a legal opinion before deciding whether to repeal the ordinance or put it to voters. Mayor blamed 'outsiders' for the pushback, fueling further controversy.

Sedgwick County preparing to extend its 90-day moratorium (set to expire April 17) to June 11, 2026. Two data center town halls scheduled: March 12 listening session and March 31 planning department session on zoning protocols. Extension proposal expected March 4.

Opposition escalating: (1) Mooringsport Mayor and residents filed lawsuit in Caddo District Court challenging Shreveport's data center permit for TXLAAR LLC, alleging NDA violations and lack of transparency; Sierra Club joining as co-plaintiffs. (2) North Caddo Parish communities organized boycott of March 3 data center meeting in Blanchard. (3) State Rep. Danny McCormick demanding transparency on NDA agreements signed by local officials. (4) Caddo Commission declined to vote on Commissioner Kracman's proposed one-year water moratorium for Caddo Lake. (5) Amazon announced $12B investment for NW Louisiana data centers, promising to limit water use.
